questions.stem: A Mobius transformation of the Riemann sphere has which general form, with $ad - bc \neq 0$?
questions.explanation: A Mobius transformation is $z \mapsto (az + b)/(cz + d)$ with $ad - bc \neq 0$. These maps form a group under composition.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The group of all Mobius transformations is isomorphic to which quotient group?
questions.explanation: Scaling the matrix to determinant $1$ gives a homomorphism from $\mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{C})$ onto the Mobius group with kernel $\{I, -I\}$. So the Mobius group is $\mathrm{PSL}(2, \mathbb{C})$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: In the convention that circles include straight lines, a Mobius transformation maps any circle on the Riemann sphere to which kind of curve?
options: An ellipse that is not a circle.
A circle on the Riemann sphere.
questions.explanation: Mobius transformations map circles to circles (where circle means a genuine circle or a straight line). This follows because circles satisfy an equation of the form $a_0|z|^2 + \overline{a}z + a\overline{z} + a_\infty = 0$ preserved by the maps.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Given any two ordered triples of distinct points on the Riemann sphere, how many Mobius transformations map the first triple to the second?
questions.explanation: There is a unique Mobius transformation sending one ordered triple of distinct points to another. This three-point uniqueness underlies much of the theory.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For the Mobius transformation $T : z \mapsto (az + b)/(cz + d)$ with $c \neq 0$, what is the image $T(\infty)$?
questions.explanation: As $z \to \infty$, the ratio $(az + b)/(cz + d) \to a/c$. So $T(\infty) = a/c$, and $T(-d/c) = \infty$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A non-identity Mobius transformation has how many fixed points on the Riemann sphere?
options: It can have three or more.
questions.explanation: A non-identity Mobius transformation has either one fixed point (parabolic case) or two fixed points. Fixed points correspond to eigenvectors of the representing matrix.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Using a representing matrix $M$ with $\det M = 1$, a non-identity Mobius transformation is parabolic exactly when which condition on the trace holds?
questions.explanation: With determinant $1$, the transformation is parabolic if and only if $\mathrm{tr}\, M = \pm 2$. It then has a single fixed point and is conjugate to $z \mapsto z + 1$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a matrix $M$ with $\det M = 1$, a non-identity Mobius transformation is elliptic exactly when the trace satisfies which condition?
questions.explanation: The transformation is elliptic exactly when $\mathrm{tr}\, M$ is real with $-2 < \mathrm{tr}\, M < 2$. It is then conjugate to $z \mapsto e^{i\theta} z$, a rotation of the sphere.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a matrix $M$ with $\det M = 1$, a Mobius transformation is hyperbolic exactly when the trace satisfies which condition?
questions.explanation: The transformation is hyperbolic exactly when $\mathrm{tr}\, M$ is real with $|\mathrm{tr}\, M| > 2$. It is then conjugate to $z \mapsto kz$ for some real $k > 0$, $k \neq 1$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A Mobius transformation with representing matrix $M$ ($\det M = 1$) is loxodromic exactly when which condition holds?
questions.explanation: The transformation is loxodromic exactly when $\mathrm{tr}\, M$ is not real. It is conjugate to $z \mapsto kz$ with $k$ complex, $|k| \neq 1$, and moves points along logarithmic spirals.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A Mobius transformation is an isometry of the Riemann sphere for the chordal metric exactly when it is represented by a matrix in which group?
options: The group of upper triangular matrices.
questions.explanation: By Proposition 8.4 a Mobius isometry of the sphere is represented by a matrix in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$, that is one with $d = \overline{a}$, $c = -\overline{b}$, and $|a|^2 + |b|^2 = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Stereographic projection from the Riemann sphere to the plane has which geometric property?
options: It maps every circle to a straight line.
It is conformal, preserving the angle between curves.
questions.explanation: Stereographic projection is conformal: it preserves the angles between curves. It also carries circles on the sphere to circles or lines in the plane.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Apply $T : z \mapsto 1/z$ to the point $z = 2$. What is $T(2)$?
questions.explanation: Direct substitution gives $T(2) = 1/2$. This map is the Mobius transformation with matrix $\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right)$ and sends $0 \mapsto \infty$, $\infty \mapsto 0$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Apply the Mobius transformation $T : z \mapsto \dfrac{z - i}{z + i}$ to the point $z = i$. What is $T(i)$?
questions.explanation: Substituting gives $T(i) = (i - i)/(i + i) = 0/(2i) = 0$. This transformation maps the upper half-plane to the unit disc, sending $i$ to the centre $0$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The characteristic equation of a $2 \times 2$ matrix $M$ with determinant $1$ is which polynomial in $t$?
questions.explanation: With $\det M = 1$, the characteristic equation is $t^2 - (\mathrm{tr}\, M) t + 1 = 0$. The trace therefore determines the eigenvalues, and hence the conjugacy class of the transformation.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An elliptic Mobius transformation is conjugate to which standard map, illustrating how it acts on the sphere?
options: $z \mapsto e^{i\theta} z$, a rotation of the sphere.
questions.explanation: An elliptic transformation is conjugate to $z \mapsto e^{i\theta} z$, which rotates the sphere fixing $0$ and $\infty$ and moves each point along a circle.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The Mobius transformations mapping the unit disc $D$ onto itself have which form?
options: $z \mapsto \dfrac{az + b}{cz + d}$ with $ad - bc = 1$ and all coefficients real.
questions.explanation: A Mobius transformation maps $D$ onto itself exactly when it has the form $z \mapsto (az + b)/(\overline{b}z + \overline{a})$ with $|a|^2 - |b|^2 = 1$. These form the group $\mathrm{Mob}(D)$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The group of Mobius transformations mapping the upper half-plane onto itself is isomorphic to which quotient?
questions.explanation: A Mobius transformation preserves the upper half-plane exactly when it is represented by a matrix in $\mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{R})$. So the group is $\mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{R})/\{I, -I\}$, the orientation preserving isometries of the hyperbolic plane.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: In the disc model, the hyperbolic density used to measure lengths is which function of $z \in D$?
questions.explanation: The hyperbolic density on the unit disc is $\lambda(z) = 2/(1 - |z|^2)$. It grows without bound as $z$ approaches the boundary circle, which is infinitely far away.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The hyperbolic geodesics of the unit disc model are which curves?
options: Straight Euclidean chords of the disc.
Arcs of circles orthogonal to the unit circle (and diameters).
questions.explanation: The hyperbolic geodesics in the disc model are arcs of circles meeting the boundary unit circle at right angles, together with the diameters through the centre.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The modular group is the group of Mobius transformations $z \mapsto (az + b)/(cz + d)$ subject to which condition on the coefficients?
questions.explanation: The modular group requires integer coefficients with $ad - bc = 1$, so it is $\mathrm{PSL}(2, \mathbb{Z}) = \mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{Z})/\{I, -I\}$. It acts as hyperbolic isometries of the upper half-plane.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The modular group acting on the upper half-plane is generated by which two transformations?
questions.explanation: The modular group is generated by the translation $A : z \mapsto z + 1$ and the inversion $B : z \mapsto -1/z$. These suffice to move any point into the standard fundamental domain.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Apply $T : z \mapsto 1/z$ to the point $z = 4$. Give the real value of $T(4)$.
questions.solution: Substituting gives $T(4) = 1/4$. As a decimal this equals {{1}}.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A Mobius transformation is represented by $M = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right)$, which has determinant $1$. Compute the trace of $M$.
questions.solution: The trace is the sum of the diagonal entries, $1 + 1$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{tr}\, M = 1 + 1 = 2$. Since the trace is $\pm 2$, this transformation is parabolic; indeed it is $z \mapsto z + 1$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A non-identity Mobius transformation is represented by a determinant-$1$ matrix with trace $0$. Classify it (parabolic, elliptic, hyperbolic, or loxodromic) using the trace criterion.
questions.solution: Since $0$ is real and $-2 < 0 < 2$, the trace criterion makes the transformation {{1}}.
questions.explanation: A real trace strictly between $-2$ and $2$ gives an elliptic transformation. Trace $0$ qualifies, so the map is elliptic (for example $z \mapsto -1/z$).
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A non-identity Mobius transformation is represented by a determinant-$1$ matrix with trace $3$. Classify it using the trace criterion.
questions.solution: Since $3$ is real and $3 > 2$, the trace criterion makes the transformation {{1}}.
questions.explanation: A real trace with absolute value greater than $2$ gives a hyperbolic transformation. Trace $3 > 2$ qualifies, so the map is hyperbolic.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups
